data.title: VYM: Real Relative Strength (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

The Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM) is an alternative offered by the market for exposure to US large caps, with a marked bias toward the dividends of those companies. To achieve this, this ETF replicates the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index, an index that encompasses a series of companies with high dividend distribution policies.

This index, part of a defined reference universe (stocks of different sizes included in the FTSE family) and uses filtering to eliminate REITs and stocks that do not pay dividends or do not project the payment of dividends at 12 months’ notice. After this, they construct a metric called 12-month forward dividend yield, to sort the companies according to their result. This is in addition to quarterly revisions to update the index if the rules indicate it. All this monitoring is carried out through the “indexation by replication” method, which consists of investing all the fund's assets in the same companies that have the index, with the same weights, in order to achieve a replica as reliable as possible.

From the point of view of the thesis and as we will see below, today I recommend maintaining VYM: the current carry of the ETF is lower than that of lower risk alternatives, so the dividend does not compensate the investor against “risk-free” instruments (see the last paragraph). At the same time, the VYM/SPY relative strength indicator remains positive even with depressed real yields (negative Z-score), suggesting that recent support does not respond to a pure “rate trade”, but to the market’s preference for quality, cash generation, and shareholder returns in a more fragile growth environment. In this context, I would only see a tactical purchase for those who seek to reduce the sensitivity of their portfolio to volatility without leaving equities, assuming that the main risk in the future is a rebound in the premium term. If the long stretch becomes stressed, the opportunity cost of maintaining a vehicle that today offers a significantly lower dividend than lower-risk alternatives increases.

ETF Overview

The table above shows the operational reality and the level of return of the ETF analyzed over time. As the reader will observe, the ETF achieves a more than efficient replica of the benchmark that it aims for, since its returns move practically in parallel (3.46% in the last month; 5.12% in 3 months; 16.20% YTD) and that of the NAV (3.42% last month; 5.12% in 3 months; 16.31% YTD). This fits with the proposed thesis, in the sense that the fund is not properly a carry product, since it is seen that in the long term it has a double-digit historical return and consequently, the argument for maintaining it becomes a profile of companies and relative behavior, not the profitability by dividend itself.

The Portfolio

Analyzing the main features of the ETF portfolio, we can see how it is a very diversified product, with 566 positions in the portfolio today. In terms of quality and valuation, the aggregate metrics are quite reasonable for an ETF of this style. With a PER ratio of 20.4x, a P/B of 2.9x, and a profit growth of 13.7% and a turnover of 13%, the numbers are indicating that this is an ETF that has a low turnover, consistent with the monitoring of the index discussed above.

The sectoral exposure indicates that the fund predominates in the financial sector, with 21% of the equity invested today. This means that we are facing an ETF with sensitivity to the credit cycle in which it will directly influence the future labor and inflationary scenario, since they are the main catalysts to cause stress situations in the credit spreads themselves. The rest of the distribution is also very informative, with a relevant block of Technology (14%), Health (12.8%) and Industrial (12.9%), reinforcing the idea of a fund that bets on quality companies that are influenced by the real economy.

As for the top 10 holdings, they currently represent a combined weight of around 28%, with a significant bias towards the financial sector. This reinforces our idea of maintaining the vehicle, not so much for the carry, but for the companies it owns, which can make money for the reader via capital appreciation.

Payoff Profile

With the dividend card, we reaffirm that VYM is not an aggressive income ETF, but rather a dividend-biased equity ETF with a more moderate payout profile. The TTM dividend yield (2.44%) is below the median for comparable ETFs (2.78%), which fits perfectly with the proposed thesis, as the carry is currently insufficient to compete with lower-risk alternatives, meaning that the dividend is not the central argument for the position. On the positive side is the consistency of the dividend itself, which, as shown in the table, has been paying a growing dividend for 15 consecutive years.

The Peers

Comparing the peers of the VYM, we see that it is not the cheapest ETF, nor is it the largest, nor is it the best performer in all environments, but it is the one that usually fits as a simple core block for those who want dividends without complications. In the comparison table, we can see that VYM shares a very low expense ratio (0.06%) with Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD), while Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) and, above all, Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) have lower investment costs. If we look at the 1-year total return chart, we can see that VYM, VIG, and VOO are all performing at similar levels of return, which tells me that it makes sense to hold it for the type of exposure it provides, without making it a core part of the portfolio because when the market weighs growth more heavily, VOO tends to outperform VYM.

Final Thoughts

VYM Yield vs Fed Funds & 10 Yr Bond Yield

Real Yield vs Relative Strength (VYM/SPY) - Z - Score

The three metrics above reflect what has been discussed throughout the article. In the first and second metrics, we show the evolution of the yield on the 10-year US bond (red line), current interest rates (EFFR - yellow line), and the evolution of the carry that the vehicle has been paying in recent years. As readers can see, the fund's current carry is 2.4%, while the “risk-free” tranche via EFFR or 10-year bonds is rewarded with 3.64% and 4.12%, respectively. Therefore, it only makes sense to maintain the vehicle as a tactical component if readers are looking for some exposure to mature companies that pay dividends.

The third metric measures the relative strength in z-score of the ETF compared to the real yield of the 10-year bond, in this case acting as a qualitative complement to my analysis. Although real 10-year rates are low today (the “price of money” in real terms is not particularly high), VYM continues to outperform the market when compared to SPY (this is what the orange line of relative strength VYM/SPY measures). This means that the ETF is “beating” the S&P 500 in relative terms, in an environment where, in theory, the growth style should shine more. Therefore, I recommend holding, as the market is relatively rewarding large, mature companies that generate cash today and distribute part of that cash to shareholders (dividends). Thank you for reading.

data.title: GDP Prints To A Huge Upside, But 2026 Remains Unclear
data.description:
NEW YORK (December 23rd)—Gross Domestic Product, or GDP, for the Third Quarter of 2025 (2025Q3) printed this morning at a surprising 4.3%, a full 100 bps above the 3.3% consensus estimate, as well as the Atlanta Fed’s “GDP Now” estimate. The data release was delayed by the October government shutdown. Markets were largely unmoved, as Wall Street typically hedges its holdings and goes largely quiet this close to Christmas.

Real final sales to private domestic purchasers, the sum of consumer spending and gross private fixed investment, increased 3.0%.

The topline print blew away even the upper bound of our highly uncertain 4% estimate of GDP in our November public jobs report. It also exceeded the "Blue Chip" consensus tracked by the St. Louis Fed, which had a wide range of estimates, and the Atlanta Fed’s near real-time GDPNow tracker, which monitors a number of data releases to regularly revise its estimate.

Let's look at our exclusive chart of 2025 Q3 GDP by Major Component.

ANALYSIS & PROGNOSTICATION

This quarter seems to have moved past most of the distortions we saw in Net Exports ("NEX") in 2025 Q1 and Q2 as companies hedged against the Trump Tariffs that took effect in April. As the chart below illustrates, we seem to be back to a "normal" balance of trade in the Third Quarter.

As we explained, companies stocked up with imports prior to what President Trump called “Liberation Day,” the day tariffs were imposed. Since Imports are a reduction in the Net Exports (NEX) calculation, the sharp increase in imports drove the economy into slightly negative GDP in 2025Q1. But then the anomaly reversed in 2025 Q2 as extra inventories burned off and imports were reduced (thereby reducing Gross Domestic Investment from the prior quarter.)

The other components of GDP are largely unremarkable, except that Gross Domestic Investment (GDI) barely moved this quarter. That seems contrary to the news we’ve heard from the Trump Administration about inbound foreign capex investment. That said, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said we will have a “bountiful” 2026 as policies like the “Big Beautiful Bill” kick in and promised foreign companies' investments come to be realized. New capex also takes a fair amount of time to ramp up, especially for property and plant.

We’re holding to our 3% estimate of GDP for 2025Q4 and 2026Q1, with a “fudge factor” of +/- 50 bps. We’re cautious about that estimate, though, given the anomalies in timely data releases owing to the October shutdown. Watch for our December jobs report in January when the data gets to be a bit clearer.

______________________________

Note: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming, and include strategies that we would recommend for the companies of our clients. Others discuss new management strategies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions here regarding whether to buy, sell, or hold such companies, however, assume the company will not change its current practices.)

data.title: GOEX: A Late-Cycle ETF To Ride Gold
data.description:
If gold is really going through a late-cycle phase, the first instrument that comes to mind is Global X Gold Explorers ETF (GOEX). This represents the segment of gold explorers, which in my opinion is the most speculative tier of the mining segment; not surprisingly, it is known for being a highly cyclical segment and often lacking fundamental values. For this reason it is speculative, therefore tactical, especially in a phase like the current one for gold. But at the same time, in my opinion not strategic at all for a portfolio. What do I mean? Let me explain better. But let's go in order.

What is GOEX?

GOEX is a thematic, commodity-oriented ETF, non-leveraged, focused on gold explorers and developers that replicates the Solactive Global Gold Explorers & Developers Total Return Index.

Participation in this ETF is quite expensive, with an expense ratio of 0.65%, to which is added a 30-day median spread of 0.99%.

Strategy

How is this objective achieved? It starts from an investable universe made up of companies active in the exploration and development phase, not yet in consolidated production, and the index includes companies for which a final investment decision on production has not yet been made (the so-called pre-FID); then a market-cap-weighted weighting is applied. This immediately distinguishes GOEX from classic mining-themed ETFs, therefore from the so-called producers. It should be remembered that producers have revenues, have margins, and offer true operating leverage to the spot price; explorers are not operating businesses, do not pay dividends, and do not have cash flows to discount. Therefore, in my view, the return on explorers depends directly on an increase in optimism, greater risk appetite, and the willingness of others to buy the story at higher prices.

So what does GOEX have that is distinctive in practical terms?

This is a good question. In theory, explorers like GOEX fit in as late-cycle solutions, not early-cycle, precisely because they react to the boom in the price of gold, not with a linear reaction tied to fundamentals. And this is an interesting element as a starting point for the reasoning, because in fact, if you look at it in technical terms, even by widening the time horizon, it is still possible to find a strong parallelism with RING, which instead theoretically has a linear relationship between the price of gold and fundamentals.

Peers

A linear relationship is also present with the junior gold miners segment, a sector with which there should be greater affinity; in this sense, the parallelism with GDXJ is interesting. Here as well, there is a fairly evident positive linear relationship.

And finally, considering a volatility of 33% and its nature, at least in theory, speculative (highly), in my opinion it makes sense to compare it with levered solutions on gold miners. In this sense, the parallelism with GDXU is interesting; the latter wins in terms of performance during bullish cycles but loses over the long run due to the volatility drag typical of 3x leveraged funds.

Holding distribution

The distribution of holdings is fairly fragmented; there are 51 holdings, well distributed, where the top 10 cover about 40% of the total distribution. The search for active solutions in the exploration segment naturally implies a low Weighted Average Market Cap, here equal to $7.3 billion. Not surprisingly, the presence of mid/small-cap miners and explorers such as Hecla, Equinox, Lundin, Alamos, Eldorado, etc., is immediately noticeable. This confirms the nature of GOEX as being more similar to a venture basket than to a hedge on physical gold.

And naturally, this also entails a certain exposure to foreign markets, considering that the core of this segment is not in the US but rather in Canada and Australia, as can be seen from the geographic distribution of the ETF.

My opinion

GOEX seems to be well positioned on a theoretical level in this phase, which in my view can be considered “late cycle.”

The price of gold is at highs, while US inflation is finally starting to ease, partly justifying the last two interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. If inflation continues in this direction, real rates could benefit, making the opportunity cost of gold higher.

Although it does not justify a dump in gold, in my view it does justify the time dimension identified as “late cycle.” And the structure of GOEX should theoretically tend to outperform in technical moments like this.

Risk

At this point, I think caution is warranted, and in my view, this makes GOEX suitable for a tactical positioning but not a strategic one within a portfolio. Let me explain better...

  • GOEX is not actually driven directly by the price of gold but by the market’s propensity to speculate on the gold explorers segment.
  • So in my opinion, the performance of this segment depends heavily on risk appetite.
  • If sentiment contracts, for example in a negative phase for gold, appreciation for this segment could disappear entirely.
  • Resulting in a significant underperformance compared to RING or GLD.

In this sense, the sideways phase of gold, from 2013 to 2020, is an interesting case study to better understand the nature of GOEX.

  • In the pre-2017 bull market, the explorers segment benefited from strong capital inflows.
  • Then, in the post-2017 contraction, interest in the segment vanished, and outflows generated a latent, multi-year underperformance, both relative to RING and GLD.

And if history teaches us anything, and it often does… it is also true that in reality, net of a typical outperformance during the upswing cycle, when widening the time horizon, GOEX does not offer such competitive returns.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think that GOEX can be an interesting tactical instrument in certain expansive phases, especially to speculate during late-cycle phases like the one we are potentially experiencing now in gold. At the same time, in my view, one should not make the mistake of thinking that this can be a portfolio substitute, given its speculative nature. For this reason, I believe the appropriate rating for this instrument remains BUY considering the structural momentum, but with a strong focus on a potential reversal, which with GOEX could arrive violently.

data.title: CEF: Another Fresh High And Further Gains Likely To Follow
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the investment backdrop for precious metals - specific to gold and silver - and why I believe the Sprott Physical Gold and Silver Trust (CEF) is a viable option right now. This is a managed fund that was created to hold substantially all of its assets in physical gold and silver bullion but "without the inconvenience that is typical of a direct investment in physical gold bullion," according to the fund's website.

This is an investment option I have written about many times and often cite it as a good option for this theme. This has certainly been true in 2025 as CEF has been one of my best performing investments and has actually been soaring well above the equity market too:

Seeing these types of gains generally prompt me to take a close eye on my holdings. Sometimes these returns aren't sustainable, and I want to adjust - but sometimes they are! In this review, I will tackle the rationale for why I will continue to hold precious metals in the year ahead, and why CEF is a reasonable option to do so. The case will be made for why a Buy rating for this fund is still appropriate despite the run CEF has had in 2025.

Both Metals Hitting Fresh Highs: Understand That

I will start this review with an emphasis that this is not a cheap area of the market. Whether that is what one is looking for or not, it is important to be aware of. Both gold and silver have been on a record run in 2025 and the momentum has carried through to late December. In fact, both metals are on track for their largest annual gain in the 1970s:

What I want to get across here is whether or not to invest now is critically determined by your own perspective. One could view these levels as signs the metals are overbought and ripe for a pullback. Fair enough, I would say there, given the pace of these gains.

But the flip to that is this is a massive momentum play that isn't over yet - which is the camp I fall into. The trends that have pushed these assets to new heights have not gone away. If anything, they are accelerating. This means more gains could be on the way, and that is what I would suggest is the base case for 2026 and why I have a continued Buy rating on CEF. But I still want readers to understand that backdrop, know the risks in case a pullback occurs, and recognize that this is not a "value" play going forward.

Central Banks Have Shifted Strategies

One of the most fundamental reasons for why I want gold in particular (and CEF by extension) has to do with my desire to follow in the footsteps of the central banks around the world. The mantra of "don't fight the central banks" is one I have harped on for the last few years, and I will continue to echo that sentiment in 2026.

What I am referring to is the rising allocation of gold by central banks, not just in the developed world, but within emerging markets as well. The net result is that these central banks are shifting away from U.S. treasuries (for a variety of reasons) and into gold. This has marked a major shift in strategy and helps explain why the demand for gold has risen so substantially in recent years:

The net result here is I want to "copy" what the central banks are doing, and that has been a profitable move since I started doing so. I would also like to emphasize that this is not a short-term play in my view. Rather, it is a structural shift and one that I see myself benefiting from for a long time. As geopolitical risks make headlines, gold soars.

Similarly, many central banks are getting distrustful of holdings traditional reserves like treasuries given that developed markets have the opportunity to freeze or seize these assets in a way they can't if the physical gold is stored outside their reach (for example, think back to the 2022 seizure of Russian reserves).

This has been a catalyst to consider gold more politically neutral than other assets and can protect foreign nation's reserves if they face economic sanctions or pressure from the developed world. This is key to why I see emerging markets driving demand over the next decade, and why it will take a marked shift in this macro-environment for me to no longer be bullish on CEF.

Rising Debt Is Another Driver

One more macro-concern that I have that is a catalyst for owning hard assets is rising debt levels. As Americans, we certainly understand this! But it isn't just an American "problem" as other nations have been borrowing at record levels too. This rise has been fairly unprecedented and is a trend readers ignore that their own peril:

Simply put - this helps make the case for hard assets of many stripes. I think it improves the forward outlook for gold and silver, and CEF by extension. But these are not the only ways to "play" this theme. One could own other commodities, real estate, collectibles, etc. But I see gold (XAUUSD:CUR) especially as a time-tested way to protect one's purchasing power in a way that is hard to match. Silver's (XAGUSD:CUR) rise this year is also showing that investor appetite is insatiable for other ways to hedge against a weaker dollar, and this makes CEF a no-brainer in my view.

Discount For CEF Persists

Investors have many options to choose from when considering precious metals. I think CEF is a good option - but there are others out there and this isn't necessarily the "only" way to play this space. Still, I like the fund's strategy as a one-stop shop for holding both gold and silver, and silver's record run this year has supported this decision as opposed to owning just gold.

Secondly, CEF continues to trade at a discount on the open market. This has persisted despite investor interest in this space and the rally we have seen in 2025. The fund's discount is wider than 3% and that piques my interest given how expensive the metals are right now:

Again, I'm not saying this is a value play, but this is indeed a "cheaper" way to own gold and silver while the metals sit at all-time highs. It tells me that this is a reasonable option to consider even with prices where they are because you are getting at least some level of a deal. If, or when, this discount evaporates, I may explore other options, but while it is present, it looks attractive to me.

The Mag 7 May Have Some Cracks

Digging deeper into why I like precious metals here has to do with what else is going on in the market. As I'm sure my followers are aware, stocks in America aren't cheap! The multiples for the S&P 500 (SP500) and NASDAQ 100 (NDX) are at multi-year highs, and that doesn't leave any room for error if we are going to see more gains. This means defensive hedges could be very useful if we experience any hiccups in early next year.

And there are some potential "errors" on the horizon. The Mag 7 in the U.S. are a driving force behind market gains, and expectations are high. Yet, expenses are rising too on the backdrop of the AI-binge. Furthermore, anticipated depreciation expenses are forecasted to rise dramatically next year for key participants in the Mag 7, as illustrated in the graphic below:

I'm not suggesting one should be selling these stocks because of this one metric. Quite the contrary - I own quite a bit of these stocks, want to hope they go up!

But the point I am making is that markets are getting to be priced for perfection. And, as we know, no stock, sector, or index, is perfect. This makes me more than willing to shift some new cash into my defensive hedges. Gold and silver are such hedges, making a strong case for CEF.

Silver Is Now A "Critical Material"

A final word on silver and its importance going forward. One will want to be a silver bull if they are going to own CEF, given the fund's two-fold strategy. If you only want gold - this isn't the option for you! But the case needs to be made for why one would continue to want to own silver in the years to come, especially as it hits at levels not seen before.

Part of the reason why I am not put-off by the rise has to do with the backdrop for the metal in terms of its importance. The U.S. Department of Interior recently added it, along with nine other minerals, to the formal "List of Critical Minerals," as shown below:

This is yet another reason why I see CEF as a long-term play. The inclusive on this list will spur additional investor demand, and that tailwind could be generational given its inclusion on this important list. Further, the industrial demand for silver remains an important trend and this list highlights that fact. All in all, this is central to why I'm not letting current prices dissuade me from adding to my position.

Bottom-Line

This has been a great year for gold and silver, and thus CEF by extension. The precious metals more broadly are benefiting from increased central bank buying globally, physical demand from investors and fund managers (think the ones who manage the ETFs and CEFs), and rising geopolitical and trade risks.

With interest rates also on the decline, the case for owning gold and silver are as high as they have ever been. While current levels do make me cautious to some degree, I think more gains are very likely. I therefore continue to have a Buy rating on CEF and suggest my followers give the idea some consideration at this time.

data.title: Copper's Outlook And The Bullish Case For Southern Copper
data.description:
Copper is the leading base metal and a critical infrastructure-building block. Meanwhile, copper’s applications in green and traditional energy initiatives have increased the demand side of its fundamental equation. While EVs, wind turbines, and other green energy innovations require increasing amounts of copper, the acceleration of electrification driven by AI will only put more strain on copper supplies. In a Dec. 17, 2025, article on Mining.com, the author explained that copper’s next short is “structural, not hype.” The article started with, “Copper is heading into a structural deficit in 2026 as demand from electrification accelerates faster than new supply.”

Southern Copper Corporation (SCCO) is one of the world’s leading copper producers.

A volatile but bullish year for the red metal

COMEX copper futures prices have moved substantially higher in 2025.

The daily year-to-date continuous copper futures chart shows that the price of nearby futures rose 38.1% from $4.0265 at the end of 2024 to $5.5610 per pound on Dec. 23, 2025. The copper futures traded to a high of $5.9585 on July 24 before correcting and recovering.

Meanwhile, the monthly 30-year continuous copper futures chart highlights the pattern of higher lows and higher highs over the past three decades. Copper has not violated significant lows and critical technical support levels during the thirty-year bullish trend.

LME copper prices caught up with COMEX futures after tariffs initially distorted prices

U.S. tariffs caused volatility and choppiness in copper futures during 2025. While COMEX futures rose to new record highs in March and July, LME three-month copper forwards waited until October 2025 to eclipse the 2024 peak and rise to a new record high.

The monthly chart of three-month LME copper forwards highlights its bullish trend. LME copper rose 36% in since the end of 2024, rising from $8,768 to $11,925 per ton on Dec. 22, 2025. While the LME forwards trailed COMEX futures earlier in 2025 due to tariff distortions, they caught up with the futures during the final months of this year.

Meanwhile, copper inventories moved from LME to COMEX warehouses throughout most of 2025. While the COMEX stocks were below LME inventories at the beginning of 2025, they were nearly three times the level of the LME stocks as of Dec. 22, 2025. The flow of copper from LME to COMEX warehouses reflects the impact of tariffs on the global copper market.

Southern Copper (SCCO) is the fifth-leading copper-producing company

Southern Copper is the fifth-leading copper-producing company. While BHP (BHP), Chile’s Codelco, Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), and Glencore (GLCNF) have more production, SCCO produces more copper than Anglo American (AAUKF), the sixth top copper producer.

Southern Copper is the third-leading mining company by market capitalization. At $148.97 per share, SCCO had a market cap of $120.55 billion. SCCO is a highly liquid stock that trades an average of over 1.05 million shares daily and pays an attractive $3.57 annual dividend, yielding 2.40%.

SCCO’s company profile states:

While SCCO’s headquarters are in Phoenix, Arizona, in the U.S., its mining properties are located in Peru, Mexico, Argentina, Ecuador, and Chile.

Beating earnings estimates- Mostly positive Seeking Alpha Factor Grades

SCCO reported its latest earnings on Oct. 28, 2025.

The chart shows that rising copper prices led SCCO to beat consensus EPS and revenue forecasts in the latest quarter. The company will report next on Feb. 12, 2026, with expectations of higher EPS and revenues.

While profitability and revisions earn SCCO A+ and A-, respectively, Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, it received B grades in the growth and revisions categories. The D grade in valuation is due to the company’s relatively higher P/E ratio compared to other mining companies.

SCCO forward P/E at 28.86 compares to BHP at 13.88, RIO at 12.28, resulting in the “D” valuation grade. SCCO is not an inexpensive stock, but rising copper prices will only increase the company’s revenues over the coming year, as it did in 2025.

SCCO outperforms copper futures in 2025

Expect that trend to continue if copper prices head higher in 2026

In late December 2025, nearby COMEX copper futures were 38.1%, while LME three-month copper prices were 36% higher since the end of 2024.

The monthly SCCO chart shows the stock rose 63.3% from $91.13 at the end of 2024 to $148.81 per share on Dec. 23, 2025. SCCO outperformed copper futures and forwards in 2025.

Mining shares provide leverage to the underlying commodities during bull markets. However, falling copper prices could have the opposite impact on SCCO and other copper mining companies. Copper’s fundamentals and technicals are aligned and bullish as the market heads into 2026, and I expect SCCO to continue outperforming the futures and forwards as it makes higher highs. I rate SCCO a buy, even though the stock is at a record high in late December. However, buying SCCO on price weakness has been optimal over the past years, so I would leave plenty of room to add on any downside corrections.

data.title: How To Profit From The Coming AI Supply Shocks In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Moving into the end of this year, I want to write an article about the broader AI space, because at this point, most analysts and individual investors expect AI to stay one of the most important market themes next year. I’m one of those people, and I’ve written about this before. I think AI should remain a major engine of market growth because it keeps expanding into new layers of infrastructure.

In this piece, I want to zoom out and treat AI like an industry, not a single stock story. I want to look at the subsectors that could benefit the most, and which tickers I think are worth watching as we head into 2026. I’m excited about this topic, I follow this space constantly, and AI stocks have become one of the core categories I cover on Seeking Alpha. So let’s begin.

The AI shift in 2026

The AI landscape in 2026 is expected to shift compared to what dominated markets in 2023 through 2025. Those years were mostly about hardware, and especially about GPUs. That’s why Nvidia (NVDA) surged the way it did. Our attention has been entirely on compute, because compute was the limiting factor and the most obvious way to monetize from this AI demand.

But as we move into 2026, and data centers and chips are not surprising anyone that much, the focus should drift toward the parts of the AI process that are now restrained. In other words, the bottlenecks. Once you deploy massive amounts of GPUs and build out the data centers, the next question should be very practical - What else do you need in order to actually use all of that compute at scale?

If you want explosive growth opportunities, I suggest looking at the shortages and bottlenecks. I will mention industries that use AI as well by the end of this piece.

Memory is the most obvious bottleneck

I want to start with memory, because it’s the example of an AI-driven bottleneck that has already arrived. I’ve covered this in my Micron (MU) and Samsung (SSNLF) articles, and it’s not exactly “new news” at this point, but there is still a chance to benefit for those who missed this rally until now.

Even after the move of this industry, I still view this as an attractive setup going into 2026, because the shortage looks like a long-playing story that is expected to continue well into 2027.

AI accelerators like Nvidia’s Blackwell, and whatever follows it, require massive amounts of HBM, or high-bandwidth memory. HBM is larger than regular RAM, it’s harder to make, it’s more complex, and it pulls manufacturing capacity away from standard DRAM used in PCs and phones. So companies have to actually allocate capacity to satisfy the demand, but even if they take down the manufacturing of regular standard chips, it won’t be enough, and the shortage will be in two segments at the same time.

This is the wafer trade-off. If you don’t know what a wafer is, I break it down in detail in my first Micron piece, but the main thing you need to know is that producing HBM consumes a lot more effective capacity than standard memory, so one wafer of HBM can eat the capacity of roughly three wafers of standard DRAM.

I even saw commentary that the shortage has gotten so messy that some new PCs are being built using older memory cards, because the supply chain is getting strained and pricing is shifting. Whether that specific anecdote is happening at scale or not, still, when the industry reallocates capacity toward HBM, the rest of the memory doesn’t stay unaffected. If demand is strong and supply is constrained, prices jump very fast, and in my Samsung article, I talked about how pricing actions can look almost absurd in real time, but the reason is that buyers are not acting like normal consumer electronics buyers. These are strategic purchases. When hyperscalers and data center builders need memory to complete systems, they will pay a lot to get their hands on something that can stop their work entirely.

So, for tickers to watch here, I still put Micron high on the list, because it remains one of the most straightforward ways for U.S. investors to express the thesis, and despite the rally, I still think the valuation is not pricing in how long this cycle can stay tight if AI demand remains strong. Samsung is trickier for many U.S. investors because the clean access is usually through the OTC ticker SSNLF, and liquidity and structure are not for everyone. SK hynix (HXSC.F) is also not simple for U.S. investors, because the U.S. exposure is typically through an ADR on the OTC market (often quoted as (HXSCF) / (HXSCL) depending on the source you’re using), and it’s not the same experience as buying a large NYSE-listed name.

I’ve also seen discussions about SK hynix potentially exploring a more direct U.S. listing path, because U.S. multiples for AI-linked suppliers are way higher. If that happens, it would be a very meaningful event for sentiment and accessibility. Until then, for most U.S.-based investors, Micron remains the simplest “buy the bottleneck” play, and once again, it is still cheap.

The next bottleneck is power

After memory, the next sector that I think deserves real attention is power. The electricity side of AI is much harder to forecast, so we can’t really know how big the shortage is going to be. Even the estimates vary wildly depending on which firm you read. I’ve looked at research, including work from the World Resources Institute, and the range is very uncertain.

But what everyone agrees on is the direction. Demand is accelerating, although for years (approximately 2010-2018), electricity demand growth was flat. Then data centers turned into the major incremental load, and now AI is pushing that load into a different category entirely. We’re moving from “data centers are growing” to “data centers are becoming industrial-scale power consumers.” Just think that you can have the GPUs, you can have the racks, you can have the building, but if the grid wasn’t made for this infrastructure, this project will be delayed for a few years, until you get the power you need.

This is why we start seeing hyperscalers signing direct deals with power producers, because they want to bypass grid bottlenecks and in some cases, secure supply. I just wrote an article about Alphabet’s (GOOG) acquisition of Intersect. But, of course, the investors won’t be able to benefit from moves like that, so let’s focus on the public companies.

A name I’ve been watching in that context is Vistra (VST). It’s not “clean and perfect,” and it hasn’t always been a market darling. The balance sheet can be a point of concern depending on how you frame leverage and cash levels, but it’s exactly the kind of company that investors ignore until suddenly they don’t. In the conditions of power scarcity, that type of business can look very different from how it looked in the old “flat demand” world.

Constellation Energy (CEG) is the other obvious name, because it’s the dominant nuclear operator story in the U.S., but it has already been repriced by the market. The stock has had a massive move, but I would keep it on the watch list and wait for the pullbacks.

Talen Energy (TLN) is another one that surged this year, and it trades like a tech story even though it’s not. That’s what happens when the market decides power scarcity is the new “AI leverage.” It’s not cheap, but according to the PEG ratio, there still is about 35% upside.

Then you have the equipment layer, which is honestly one of the most interesting parts of this whole theme, because even if you build generation, you still need the hardware that moves and manages electricity. Eaton (ETN) is a clean example of a company exposed to that. If AI load growth forces grid upgrades, the backlog in this category can stretch for years. The issue, of course, is valuation. A lot of this has been bid up already, and the market is aware of the story. That’s why I keep circling back to a simple approach here: some of these names may not be “buy today and forget,” but they can be excellent on weakness if the market shifts or enters a correction, so this is another name to add to the watchlist.

Optical networking

Another area I think more people will be talking about in 2026 is optical networking. As AI models grow, workloads are distributed across many GPUs, which effectively turns parts of the data center into a supercomputer. And once you do that, you run into a very unsexy but very real constraint with connectivity.

Copper does not scale well for everything you want to do in these architectures, especially as distances and bandwidth requirements expand. That’s why the industry is switching into optical interconnects, silicon photonics, and higher-bandwidth transceivers, including 800G and 1.6T. I think this is the simplest way to explain it, is that the “optics spend” per “compute spend” rises. In other words, for every dollar you spend on GPUs, you increasingly have to spend additional dollars on the cables, transceivers, and signal processing that let those GPUs operate in racks as one system.

Marvell (MRVL) is one of the most important names to notice here, especially through its positioning in high-end DSP, which is the digital signal processing layer that helps translate and move signals in optical systems. MRVL has had a rough year, and sentiment hasn’t been kind. But when I look at the role this company plays as optical connectivity becomes more critical, it’s hard for me to dismiss it as just “another semi name.” If the shortage dynamics show up here the way they’ve shown up in other AI-adjacent supply chains, Marvell in 2026 could repeat Micron’s surge from this year.

Coherent (COHR) is another name that comes up because it’s tied to lasers and transceivers, and it’s ramping capacity in the high-bandwidth optical category. The stock has already had a huge move this year, but even in a repriced stock, you can still have upside if the demand curve stays steeper than what the market is currently assuming.

Fabrinet (FN) is also positioned well. It’s one of the companies that would benefit when the optical supply chain accelerates, because somebody has to actually build the physical products at scale.

The “regular AI winners”

Of course, not everything in AI has to be about bottlenecks. The major platform companies and the core semiconductor leaders are presumed to do well in 2026. Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia, Microsoft (MSFT), as for hyperscalers, Broadcom (AVGO), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), as for other talked-about stocks in the space.

Google is one of the names I continue to view as a long-term compounder in this space, and the direction it’s taking, including its push into energy-related strategy. Amazon remains one of my conviction holdings, because AWS is still one of the most direct ways to express the infrastructure side of AI over a long runway. Broadcom is a name I continue to like going into 2026 because the “custom silicon” trend is strong as companies want to diversify away from Nvidia, and Broadcom is one of the companies helping hyperscalers design the chips they want instead of buying everything. AMD is in an interesting stock as well. It has already repriced from where it used to be, but depending on how you frame growth versus valuation, it can still be attractive for investors who believe in the long-term competitive landscape. And Nvidia is Nvidia. If you’re a long-term holder and you believe AI infrastructure keeps expanding, it’s hard to argue that Nvidia becomes “irrelevant” anytime soon.

The “second wave” of AI

Finally, the second wave will go into the use cases for AI, although I think the real growth awaits further than the next year.

Cybersecurity, in my opinion, will be a large multi-year growth story. Companies are still figuring out how to integrate AI into workflows, and threats will evolve as adoption deepens. I don’t necessarily think 2026 is guaranteed to be the year where the whole sector suddenly explodes upward. But I do think there’s a setup forming where a lot of cybersecurity and software names are not priced like “AI beneficiaries,” even though they can become beneficiaries over time.

A lot of tech this year has been very split. Hyperscalers and AI-linked infrastructure did well, while plenty of “regular tech” lagged. SentinelOne (S) is an example of a name that has been punished, and that punishment has reasons behind it, but this can be a compelling entrance point. CrowdStrike (CRWD) is the opposite: a premium-valued leader that can still be attractive, but I would be watching to add on dips when they occur. It is a volatile name, I’ve traded it before around large drawdowns, and it’s exactly the kind of name where you don’t need to “chase.”

I also keep names like Palo Alto Networks (PANW), CyberArk (CYBR), and Fortinet (FTNT) on the radar, because as AI expands, the security perimeter doesn’t get simpler. It gets more complicated. I’ve broken down two of the first ones in a single article before.

And then you have agents. The whole “AI helps people do work” story, including code generation and copilots. Right now, this space is dominated by the hyperscalers and the major platforms, but I also think we’ll see more pure-play companies emerge over time, including ones that are not public yet. Startups are actively changing hiring strategies and relying more on tooling, so this is the area I follow, and I wait for it to become bigger.

Closing thoughts

If I had to summarize how I’m thinking about AI going into 2026, I would say that the theme is not going away, but the market’s attention will keep rotating within the theme. From 2023 to 2025, the easiest narrative was GPUs, but in 2026, I think the more interesting conversation becomes what’s holding AI back from scaling even faster.

That’s why I am following the memory, power, grid hardware, optical networking, etc. These aren’t the “sexiest” AI topics, but they are very investible, and that’s what counts.

So if you want the names that could surprise on the upside, I’d focus less on what everyone already agrees is the winner, and more on the parts of the supply chain where demand is growing faster than supply can realistically expand. Again, hyperscalers most likely will be compounders, so if you want a safer bet, look among the regular popular names.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rocket Lab: I Am Selling Half Of My Position After Investing At $6
data.description:
Almost 2 years ago, I turned bullish on Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB). The stock was trading just a tad below $6 at the time. Based on yesterday's closing price of $77.55, my initial RKLB investment is sitting on a nice gain of 1,200%+. This makes RKLB the best-performing stock in my portfolio since late 2023.

Last May, I argued why Rocket Lab may achieve positive FCF as soon as 2026 (in my model, I used a revenue estimate of $1 billion for Rocket Lab in FY 2026). In a massive boost to Rocket Lab's backlog, the company was awarded an $816 million SDA contract on Dec. 19. This is the biggest contract Rocket Lab has secured so far. But its importance extends beyond just the value of this contract. This SDA contract win marks the SDA's recognition of Rocket Lab as a prime contractor for the first time. For those who are not familiar with the term, a prime contractor is responsible for the entire scope of a mission, from designing the satellites to building them to managing payloads. Previously, Rocket Lab collaborated with federal agencies in the capacity of a subcontractor. As a prime, Rocket Lab is joining an elite group of select few companies such as Lockheed Martin (LMT), Northrop Grumman (NOC), and L3Harris (LHX).

Despite all these positive developments since my last article in May (and a 225% increase in the stock price), I have decided to trim my position today based on an objective view of the company's current valuation. This does not mean that I am turning into a bear. However, as I explain later in this analysis, I will be holding on to half of my RKLB position for the long term as I continue to believe in the company's disruptive potential (from a mathematical perspective, the acquisition cost of these shares is virtually zero as I have already recouped more than my original investment).

Vertical Integration Is Paying Off

Before I come to the valuation of Rocket Lab, I thought it best to share my views on how Rocket Lab's vertical integration efforts are paying off. In the long run, I believe vertical integration will go a long way in helping Rocket Lab's earnings growth as it transforms into a prime contractor.

Rocket Lab has been touting its vertical integration efforts in the past few years, but it has certainly taken some time for investors to truly appreciate the economic value of these efforts. At an operational level, there are clear signs that Rocket Lab's vertical integration efforts are delivering desired results. A good example is how Rocket Lab would be utilizing technology from 3 different acquisitions it completed in the recent past for its new SDA contract.

  1. Acquired SolAero, a space solar cell structure manufacturer, for $80 million in 2022.
  2. Acquired Advanced Solutions, a flight software company, for $40 million in 2021.
  3. Acquired Sinclair Interplanetary, a satellite hardware manufacturer, in 2020.

As the below slide from the Q3 earnings presentation confirms, Rocket Lab is not slowing down with its acquisition spree.

Exhibit 1: Q3 acquisition updates

For a prime contractor, operational and cost efficiency is key to generating a profit from awarded contracts, as these contracts are often awarded at a fixed price. The contract winner (the prime) is responsible for delivering the deliverables on time and bears the financial risks arising from higher-than-expected expenses.

Rocket Lab, as a result of its acquisition spree leading up to 2025, does not have to involve a third party for solar panels, satellite manufacturing, or space systems software. Therefore, I believe the company is well-positioned to control its cost structure better while acting as a prime contractor for the SDA.

Rocket Lab's improving gross margins also highlight the progress of its vertical integration strategy. In the aerospace industry, gross margin improvements are very difficult to book as long as a company is leaving money on the table with third-party suppliers and middlemen. Rocket Lab's GAAP gross margin reached 37% in Q3. As illustrated below, gross margins have come a long way since 2020 (-34%).

Exhibit 2: Gross margin growth

I believe vertical integration has played a key role in this gross margin expansion. From reaction wheels to other major hardware and software components, Rocket Lab usually builds them. This removes the middleman's cut (compared to if Rocket Lab were outsourcing the manufacturing of these parts).

The Valuation Math Is Not Mathing On A Few Fronts

Investing is not just about finding great companies. If that were the case, it would have been very difficult to lose money in the stock market (unless you treated the market as a place to gamble). The relatively easy part (at least for me) is identifying companies that seem well-positioned to grow. The difficult part for me is attaching a justifiable fair value to these companies so that I have a reference framework to determine if a company is seemingly undervalued or overvalued (seemingly because I would never know for sure).

When Rocket Lab was trading at $6 a couple of years ago, it was an easy decision for me based on my analysis that showed Rocket Lab was well on track to dominate the small payload market (which it has). Today, at a market cap of $38 billion, Rocket Lab may not sound like a big company but is valued at a forward P/S of 62x. This makes the company far from cheaply valued. Unlike 2 years ago, Rocket Lab is no longer a hidden gem in the market.

It is common to see P/S multiples of 50x+ among SaaS companies that are scaling fast. A good example is Palantir Technologies (PLTR), which is trading at a forward P/S multiple of 104x. I am not going to discuss whether Palantir is richly valued or not here, but the point is that fast-growing SaaS businesses often attract high P/S multiples. Rocket Lab is also growing fast. However, there is a key differentiator between a manufacturing-heavy company like Rocket Lab and a SaaS business like Palantir. SaaS businesses often enjoy substantially higher gross margins compared to a manufacturing-heavy business. In Q3, Palantir reported gross margins of 82%. For the same period, Rocket Lab's gross margin was just north of 37%. Make no mistake, Rocket Lab's gross margin of 37% makes the company a leader in its league, given that legacy prime contractors such as Lockheed Martin often see gross margins of around 10%-12%. Given the two different structures of a fast-growing SaaS business and a fast-growing space company (space companies require a lot of manufacturing efforts to grow), Rocket Lab cannot permanently trade at SaaS multiples. This is a major red flag. And this is one of the main reasons why I have decided to sell half of my position today.

There is more to the valuation story.

Rocket Lab, with Electron, has solidified its dominant position in the small payload market. None of its competitors is posing a threat to Rocket Lab at this point. A quick look at the average annual launches of small payload rockets confirms that Electron is in a league of its own. I included this table in my previous analysis, but for better context, it makes sense to refer to this table in this analysis too.

Rocket name Operator Average annual launches in the recent past Notes
Electron Rocket Lab 10-15 Most frequently launched small rocket in the world
Ceres-1 Galactic Energy 3-6 Leading small payload launcher in China
Vega Arianespace 2-4 One of the leading small payload launchers in Europe
Long March 6/11 CASC 2-5 A leading small payload launcher in China
Pegasus Northrop Grumman less than 1 Launch services have been scrapped largely
LauncherOne Virgin Orbit 2 Highly infrequent launches

This market dominance is already baked into the valuation of Rocket Lab. That has to be expected. Where things turn a bit gloomy from a valuation perspective is that the expected success of Neutron also seems baked into the current valuation of the company. If Neutron succeeds in 2026 (the first launch is planned for Q1 2026), we are looking at Rocket Lab competing with the well-established market leader in the large payload segment, SpaceX. There is room for both of these players in the large payload market, as SpaceX's current capacity does not seem to be sufficient to handle the demand for large payloads today. However, the problem is that Neutron may face several setbacks before finally entering commercial operations. Unfortunately, Rocket Lab's current valuation does not seem to account for this possibility. This creates a massive fallout risk for investors in case the Neutron launch is delayed (in the space industry, this is a common occurrence).

I still believe in Neutron's potential to convert the large payload market into a duopoly alongside SpaceX in the long run (currently, this market is more of a monopoly due to a lack of healthy competition). However, I do not want to bet on this happening in 2026 itself. I would rather prefer buying more time given the volatility of the space industry. This is why I will be offloading half of my RKLB position in the coming days.

The remaining half, as you may understand, will give me enough exposure to Rocket Lab's long-term growth potential without the headaches of having to worry about my investment outlay, as I would be literally banking almost 7x my initial investment by liquidating only half of my holding. Moving forward, I will think of my remaining exposure to RKLB as cost-free exposure.

Takeaway

Rocket Lab stock has performed better than I expected in the past couple of years. While continuing to believe in the company's disruptive potential, I have decided to sell half of my position in light of its expensive valuation. To better reflect my current stance on the company's valuation, I am migrating to a "Hold" stance on RKLB (I previously had a "Buy" rating on the company).

data.title: Exxon Mobil Remains Woefully Underestimated
data.description:
Back in May of this year, I lauded Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) for the prospect of strong growth that the company had been accomplishing. Overall, the picture for the company looked good, with profitability leading up to that point rising compared to the same time a year earlier. But more important than that, thanks largely in part to acquisition activities, specifically its purchase of Pioneer Natural Resources in a deal that cost shareholders $68 billion on an enterprise value basis, the future looked bright. Management had been forecasting significant production increases over the next several years. And with that, the company would capture around $6 billion in structural cost savings.

Despite these positives, the company has fallen short of the "Buy" rating that I assigned it. You see, whenever I rate a company a "Buy," I expect the stock to outperform the market for the foreseeable future. But since then, shares are up only 8.9%, while the S&P 500 is up 17.3%. Given the amount of time that has passed and this underperformance, I wanted to revisit the picture. After all, nobody is perfect, and I could be wrong in my assessment. But after looking at the most recent data that management has provided, and the promise that it has offered of better days ahead, Exxon Mobil is still an appealing candidate.

A Great Play On Future Growth

Instead of focusing on the past, I think that for this article, the best emphasis is on the future. And this is because management has been very detailed regarding what they see going on moving forward. For instance, on Dec. 9, management provided a multiyear outlook that lays out where they think the company is heading. One thing that is clear is that management expects to significantly boost earnings and cash flow. For context, back in 2024, Exxon Mobil generated net income of $33.68 billion.

They expect to grow that by $25 billion by the year 2030. That would take net profits up that year to an astounding $58.68 billion. But that's nothing. Management expects that operating cash flow will grow from $55.02 billion in 2024 to roughly $90.02 billion by the end of the decade. These increases in profitability also represent an upward revision compared to what management previously thought. In the past, they expected earnings to only grow by $20 billion. And they expected cash flow to grow by roughly $30 billion.

Management's optimism centers around two different themes that the company is pursuing. First and foremost would be cost savings. In the past, Exxon Mobil was, according to management's own description, a very siloed business. In the image above, you can see precisely what I mean. But by 2022, the company had transformed into three main sets of operations, all under one umbrella. These kinds of restructuring activities have paved the way for cost reductions. For instance, instead of having over 10 functional ERP systems that created inefficiencies, the company has a single enterprise-wide solution. It plans to make other changes as well as time goes on, including through investments in additional technologies. Ultimately, the company expects that, compared to 2019, it will capture $20 billion in structural cost savings. Their previous forecast called for this to be $18 billion.

Of course, it is worth noting that most of those cost savings have already been baked into the company's financial picture. That's where the other side of profit increase is expected to come from. This involves earnings growth through production growth. You see, right now, the total potential addressable markets that the company plays in will be worth an estimated $4 trillion by the year 2030. And with global population growth expected to continue, and with certain catalysts driving energy demand higher, the company expects that market size to double to $8 trillion by the year 2050. This is no longer an oil company. In fact, it hasn't been for a while now. Management has been gradually transitioning this into a business that touches on all aspects of the economy, including things like the medical space, agriculture, emissions reduction activities, electronics, construction, mobility, packaging, and more.

When it comes to the Upstream operations of the company, management expects to achieve around $14 billion of earnings growth from 2024 through 2030. While cost reductions will play a role here, overwhelmingly the increase will come from higher volumes and changes in product mix. Management expects, for instance, for overall production to climb from 4.3 Moebd to 5.5 Moebd. That's actually up from the previous expected end point of 5.4 Moebd. And on top of this, the expected unit earnings per barrel have been revised higher by $2. I know that doesn't sound like much. But at scale, it is massive.

Another big driver of this earnings growth will be its Product Solutions business. This is the part of the company that consists of its energy products, chemical products, and specialty products businesses. For context, energy products include fuels, aromatics, catalysts, and certain licensing activities. Chemical products include olefins, polyolefins, and the like. And specialty products include things like finished lubricants, base stocks, synthetics, elastomers, and resins. What these all have in common is that these are value-added petroleum products and petrochemicals. In essence, management takes the products of its Upstream operations and refines those products into more valuable end goods.

This part of the company's management expects to see at least $9 billion of earnings growth from 2024 through 2030. $4 billion of this will come from advantaged projects, with 60% de-risked from completed projects already. And according to management, over 40% of the earnings growth coming from these operations will involve high-value products that the company continues to increase its emphasis on. The goal here is not just to capture additional revenue. It's to capture high-margin revenue.

We can also expect changes when it comes to its Low Carbon Solutions business. The entire reason for this unit is to build out a pipeline of emission reduction opportunities involving carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emissions fuels, lithium for the global battery and electric vehicle markets, and more. Management hasn't really provided a lot of detail here. But we do know already that its carbon capture and storage business is already significant. And when you look at all of its new businesses that are included under its corporate segment, the company expects to eventually grow earnings to $13 billion by the year 2040.

Not surprisingly, none of this will come cheap. Management is making significant investments to achieve these outcomes. This year, for instance, including cash acquisitions, the firm is expected to spend $29 billion on capital initiatives. In 2026, we can expect between $27 billion and $29 billion. And for the 2027 through 2030 fiscal years, we can expect between $28 billion and $32 billion annually. An increasing portion of capital expenditures will go toward early-stage major projects and early-stage policy-dependent and new business activities. And management expects that around $20 billion of lower emissions investments will be made from this year through 2030.

Projecting out what the future of the company will look like is difficult even with all of this factored in. But the good news is that management has given us some things to work with. They expect, for instance, to continue around $20 billion of share buybacks annually through next year. And on top of this, they also have provided a range of how much total cash flow the company should generate from its operating activities for the 2026 through 2030 fiscal years. For instance, if Brent oil prices average $55 per barrel, we can expect around $385 billion of cumulative cash flow. Around $250 billion is what the company needs to cover cash capital expenditures and its existing dividend. So this would leave an excess of $135 billion for shareholders. If oil averages $65 per barrel, excess will be $145 billion. And if it averages $85 per barrel, we can expect $245 billion in excess.

In the near term, I actually expect prices to go closer to the $55 range. Potentially even lower. And that's because of growing U.S. production and weakening economic conditions. But over the long haul, I believe that oil belongs somewhere between $60 and $80 per barrel. So for the purpose of this analysis, I will assume that the $65 per barrel is what ultimately is realized. Following this approach, we can see in the table below how much in annual operating cash flow, capital expenditures, share buybacks, and how much will be left between dividends and extra cash, the company should have through the end of this decade.

To have everything aligned, I did have to assume that, at some point throughout this time, the company would sell off assets worth about $7.26 billion. That's relatively small in the grand scheme of things. And it would not be uncharacteristic of the company to unload non-core assets. But because there is timing uncertainty, I assumed that this would happen at the very end of the forecast. In the table below, you can also see what we would be looking at in terms of changes in share count, the implied market capitalization of the company based on its current trading multiple, the implied price per share of the business, and how much total cash would come to shareholders between dividends and extra cash sitting on the company's books. You can also see total implied upside and implied annual upside. On an annualized basis, we are looking at an upside of around 15% from where the stock is right now. Considering that the S&P 500 typically averages around 10% or 11% a year, you can understand my optimism.

Takeaway

The way I see things, absent a plunge in energy prices that keeps prices low for an extended period, Exxon Mobil makes for a compelling prospect. The company is making big changes, as it has in the past. And if management is right, the future looks bright. Even if the trading multiples of the company remain unchanged from where they are right now, we are looking at market-beating potential. But I suspect that if management does start to deliver in a big way on this guidance, we could see further upside from an expansion of its trading multiples. Given this, I have no problem maintaining my "Buy" rating on the stock.

data.title: Moderna: Why I Am Selling The 'Relief Rally'
data.description:
Introduction

Moderna, Inc.’s stock (MRNA) has rallied ~23% since my last Sell rating in August.

Although I acknowledged the long-term potential of its CMV and personalized cancer vaccines, the company was burning $2-3 billion per year despite cost reductions. Meanwhile, its COVID-19 vaccine franchise, which made “Moderna” a household name, was in decline. Finally, I believed that Moderna’s other main product, mRESVIA (RSV), was likely to take a back seat to much larger competitors such as GSK plc (GSK) and Pfizer (PFE).

Moderna’s recent rally has me wondering if it has any “legs.” The article that follows reveals that Moderna’s fundamentals have arguably worsened since August, with the most recent rally likely being attributed to improved liquidity and cost discipline.

Financial Health

In November, Moderna released its Q3 earnings report, which featured healthy beats on revenue ($1.02 billion beats by $150 million) and EPS (-$0.51 beats by $1.67). The revenue beat was thanks to Moderna’s COVID vaccine, which generated $971 million in Q3. Moderna’s RSV vaccine, mRESVIA, brought in a paltry $2 million in sales. For reference, Moderna’s peers, Pfizer and GSK, generated $279 million and £0.3 billion (roughly $400 million) in Q3, respectively, for their RSV vaccines.

Despite the Q3 beat, Moderna narrowed its full-year 2025 revenue guidance and lowered the top end of US revenue guidance. Management also explicitly stated that they do not see US vaccination rates returning to flat, which caps any “rebound” narrative. And, make no mistake about it, while cash burn has lessened, it's still significant. Per Seeking Alpha, cash from operations (a proxy for cash burn) was ($847 million) in Q3.

Moderna has been making honest efforts to extend its cash runway. Its year-end cash and investments projection was increased from $6 billion to $6.5 billion to $7 billion. In late November, Moderna secured $1.5 billion in non-dilutive financing and, again, increased its year-end cash and investment projection to between $7.1 billion and $7.6 billion. Moreover, the company announced that it would cut four pipeline programs, including congenital CMV (as well as HSV, VZV, and GSD1a), signaling further cost savings.

This combination of lower 2025 operating expense guidance, a higher 2025 year-end cash balance, and the $1.5 billion loan materially reduces near-term dilution pressure. I suspect that this is the reason for Moderna’s recent stock rally.

Recent Developments

While CMV falling can be good for the cash story, it is bad for Moderna's intrinsic pipeline value. Discontinuing CMV removes what was previously lauded as a potential billion-dollar class vaccine.

As I highlighted in my last Moderna write-up, CMV was a key near-term vaccine growth pillar, and something that could have made me optimistic on the name had the program succeeded. Without it, Moderna’s dependence on the remaining respiratory franchise and the Merck-partnered (MRK) oncology program increases.

Simultaneously, mNEXSPIKE share gains do not solve the core demand constraint. As the company noted in its Q3 earnings call, “cumulative retail vaccinations were 13.2 million, down approximately 30% year-over-year.” In essence, Moderna’s COVID franchise is fighting for a larger slice of a smaller pie. Eventually, this will catch up to it.

Critically, Moderna’s Q3 mNEXSPIKE performance requires some nuance. Management’s seasonal working capital explanation for its Q3 cash decline implies front-loaded channel fill dynamics. Basically, Q3 revenue is predominantly a feature of build, rather than actual demand, that must convert into “shots in the arm,” which usually happen during Q4 (vaccination season).

The company admitted they are deferring Phase 3 spend in infectious disease until after breakeven and are seeking partners for Phase 3 assets like EBV. Again, while this stretches its cash, it effectively says it cannot or will not self-fund large pivotal programs in the near term, increasing its dependence on partners.

Moderna’s own 2028 cash breakeven projection appears to hang more on cost savings than it does revenue acceleration. The only near-term revenue levers management was able to cite were contracted government partnerships and geography, not new product ramps. This implies that Moderna is prioritizing survival over growth visibility.

Final Words

To wrap up, Moderna’s recent stock rally looks like a “sigh of relief” from market participants. Moderna has effectively extended its cash runway and is no longer in imminent danger.

However, if you take a step back, the core, long-term problems are still there and have, arguably, deteriorated since my last look. The COVID vaccination market is in perpetual decline. Although mNEXSPIKE is making up for this in the interim by securing more market share, this cruel reality remains. Moreover, Moderna’s mRESVIA has to settle for the “back of the line” in a highly competitive RSV vaccine market. Once hoped to reinvigorate its infectious disease pipeline, congenital CMV has been "thrown in the trash" in an effort to boost Moderna's near-term financial outlook.

So, these “positive” financial developments must be viewed in a proper context. Moderna is essentially cannibalizing itself in an effort to be lighter and faster. However, I propose that this type of strategy is unlikely to win the long-distance races inherent in drug development.

Net-net, despite the apparent "relief rally," I am still cautious on Moderna's long-term prospects (maintain “Sell” and “market underperform”).

Some things that could change my mind include U.S. COVID vaccination stabilization, mRESVIA showing material traction, flu mRNA 1010 and flu plus COVID mRNA 1083 achieving approvals and demonstrating real demand, and a quantitatively supported bridge to 2028 cash breakeven that relies on revenue drivers instead of mere cost cuts.

data.title: Intel's Most Unusual Bull Case
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intel Corporation’s (INTC) investment case is increasingly anchored in U.S. industrial policy rather than near-term fundamentals. Massive government backing, alongside strategic capital from partners like Nvidia (NVDA), de-risks its costly foundry pivot and shores up the balance sheet.

INTC has already rerated meaningfully since earlier coverages, yet the stock has largely stalled since my last coverage in October, trading in a holding pattern as the market waits for a clear, next catalyst to justify another leg higher.

While execution risks and foundry losses persist, Intel’s role as a national semiconductor champion reframes timelines, attracts patient capital, and improves the odds that today’s cash burn evolves into long-term strategic value.

U.S. Policy Backing Injects Billions, De-Risks Intel Foundry Pivot

The main bullish factor for Intel is its deepening integration with the United States' national industrial and security policy. It started as a matter of getting a government grant, and now Intel is repositioning as a crown jewel of American tech sovereignty.

This positioning brings in capital, de-risks an expensive business model transition, and creates a market theme that can attract more deals and government favor beyond near-term financial issues. So my analysis begins with the direct capital infusions that are reshaping Intel's balance sheet. There is accelerated funding from the United States government, and there are important investments from Nvidia and SoftBank Group (SFTBY).

In Q3 alone, Intel received $5.7 billion from the US government. Back in August, the Trump Administration invested $11.1 billion for a ~10% stake in Intel. This influx of capital makes Intel a top priority for the government through shoring up the balance sheet. Intel repaid $4.3 billion of debt in Q3. The government's direct financial backing directly addresses Intel’s point of financial vulnerability and provides Intel with the cash to pursue its capital-intensive foundry track without facing historical market pressures.

I believe this financial backing is a result of Intel’s central role in national actions. For me, the Genesis Mission is a big example. Intel is named as one of 24 companies partnering with the U.S. Department of Energy to accelerate discovery science, solidify national security, and energy advancements. The mission’s aim is to build the national AI platform for scientific discovery.

Thus, Intel is now a foundational partner in a national strategic endeavor. This positions Intel at the core of the U.S.'s response to global competition in AI, manufacturing, and energy. CEO Lip-Bu Tan confirmed this on the Q3 call.

I'm honored by the trust and confidence President Trump and Secretary Lutnick have placed in me. support highlights Intel's strategic role as the only US-based semiconductor company with leading-edge logic R&D and manufacturing.

Moreover, he stated, Intel is

fully committed to advancing the Trump administration's vision to restore semiconductor production.

Further, the current positioning of Intel supports Wall Street’s bullish tone for Intel's highly expensive project (the Intel Foundry). The semiconductor manufacturing is mostly concentrated at TSMC (TSM). There will be bad outcomes for the U.S. tech sector if China takes action against Taiwan. So, the chip production outside of Taiwan by Intel is essential for a diversified supply for the U.S. tech sector.

The U.S. government's investment and vocal backing are clearly targeting building a domestic, at-scale, leading-edge alternative to TSMC. Intel Foundry is that alternative. Lip-Bu Tan has a plan to "work with the U.S. government within the secure enclave." Overall, the entire foundry project is framed as a matter of national economic security, which makes its success a government priority.

Beyond policies, my analysis of Intel's Q3 and its outlook points to several bullish signals. The most immediate is the massive earnings beat in Q3. Intel reported revenue of $13.65 billion and a non-GAAP EPS of $0.23, beating the EPS consensus estimate by a massive magnitude of $0.01. The outcome shows that there would have been a much more superior performance.

I also see a much-improved financial position with $30.9 billion in cash after repaying $4.3 billion of debt. This reflects my point that injection of capital from the government and partner investments provides Intel a solid footing to fund its operational and CapEx plans.

For the outlook, the forecast for the PC market is also encouraging. The client consumption TAM may hit 290 million units in 2025, which is the fastest TAM growth since 2021. This points to a healthy demand for Intel's largest business segment. The new collaboration with Nvidia also opens a new avenue for growth by combining Intel’s CPUs with Nvidia's NVLink tech. It is positioning Intel to address new parts of the AI market (like AI PCs).

Foundry Burns $2.3 Billion Quarterly, Delaying Profitability Into 2026

As I review the financials and management's own commentary in the Q3 call, I see a big weakness still there for Intel. There is a disconnect between the strategic importance of the Intel Foundry and its current operations and financials. The entire turnaround story and the justification for its national champion status lie in the foundry’s business success.

Yet this segment is currently a large financial drain with clear execution challenges to its profitability. The most direct pattern of this problem is in the numbers, as Intel Foundry has an operating loss of $2.3 billion in Q3. This loss was on revenue of $4.2 billion for the segment.

An operating loss of this size points to the fact that the business is actively burning through large amounts of cash. This is sharply against the Intel products segment that generated $3.7 billion in operating profit. It means the new strategic direction of Intel is currently erasing a large portion of the profits from its legacy business. The gross profit margin of 38% improved YoY, but the foundry's high unprofitability works as a drag on the bottom line.

Further, the execution on manufacturing yields for Foundry’s new technologies is not yet at an economically viable level. The yields can address the supply, but they are not at the level to drive up the margins. As per the CFO, it may take a year. This is a direct admission that for at least the next year, the core tech of the foundry may not be profitable on a per-unit basis. This is an execution risk, as the entire financial model of a foundry depends on high-yielding and cost-effective manufacturing.

Intel is signaling that this is still a work in progress. As per the CFO, the forecast for a lower gross margin in Q4 of 36.5% is partly due to the impact of the first Core Ultra 3 shipments, which have a higher cost than in the early stages of product ramp.

As Intel tries to move its products to its own new advanced foundry nodes like 18A, I think it will continue to face margin dilution in the upcoming quarters. Why? These nodes are expensive and have lower yields initially. Intel is in a difficult position where its stock price upside depends on the market adoption of these new nodes, as slow adoption will hurt its short-to-mid-term profitability.

Finally, this problem is compounded by capacity limits on its older, more mature, and presumably more profitable manufacturing processes. Capacity constraints on Intel 10 and Intel 7 limited Intel from fully meeting demand in Q3 for both data center and client markets. This means that at the same time its new foundry processes are unprofitable and dilutive, Intel cannot satisfy demand for its older, more cost-effective chips.

Takeaway

Intel is no longer just a turnaround bet; it’s a policy-backed strategic asset. Government capital lowers financial risk, extends the runway, and shifts investor focus from quarterly pain to long-term relevance. Execution still matters, but national priority status meaningfully tilts the risk-reward balance in Intel’s favor.
